Add Example Project

Example projects are generated by TestRail and contain sample data such as test cases, results and milestones. They are a good way to see and use many of TestRail's features and are perfect for evaluation or demonstration purposes.

  • Ideal for reports with interactive elements such as hover effects or expandable tables. (Note: Email provides may block HTML Reports)
  • Best for large Reports for best performance.
  • Best for small Reports

    Delete Cancel
    Cancel Test Case Generation

    Canceling will stop the test case generation process. Any progress made, including the AI credits used so far, will be lost. Do you want to proceed?

    Yes, Cancel No, Keep
    Warning: Unsaved Changes

    Cancelling will prevent the automation test from being linked to the test case, and all progress, including chat history, will be lost. Do you want to proceed?

    No, Stay Yes, Cancel
    Confirmation

    You are about to delete the automation test. This will permanently remove it from the test case. Do you want to continue?

    No, Keep Yes, Delete
    Replace Existing Automation Test?

    You're about to add a new automation test. This will permanently replace the existing one, and the previous version cannot be recovered. Do you want to continue?

    Yes, Replace No, Discard
    Warning: Test scenarios will be updated.

    Editing requirements and regenerating will replace all existing generated test cases with new ones. Are you sure you want to continue editing?

    Continue Cancel
    1/6 Create a Project

    Welcome! Start by creating your first project. Projects in TestRail usually represent a product or software project and serve as organizational unit for cases, results and milestones. You can create as many projects as you like.

    2/6 Add Test Cases

    Test cases represent a certain feature, behavior or functionality you wish to test. They often contain a description, a list of steps and expected results. Cases are organized in sections to make it easy to group related cases together.

    3/6 Start a Test Run

    To execute tests and enter results for your cases, you start a test run. You can have multiple test runs over time and reuse your test cases across runs. A test run has a status and you can easily follow its progress and test activity.

    4/6 Add Test Results

    Once you've added a test run, you can start testing and record results. A result has a status such as Passed or Failed and the statuses are signaled by different colors. Adding results contributes to the overall status and progress of the test run.

    5/6 Invite Team Members

    TestRail is especially useful when used with a team. Invite other team members and build your test case repository, assign test runs and record results together. Easily track the workload and progress from the Todo tab for your entire team.

    6/6 Set up Integration

    Integrate TestRail with your existing tools such as issue or bug trackers (such as Jira), requirement management tools, wiki software and many more. Collaborate with your dev team, run traceability reports and link cases & results to issues and defects.
    1. Create a project
    2. Add cases
    3. Start a run
    4. Add results
    5. Invite users
    6. Set up integration
    {{attachment_truncated_name}}
    1/6 Create a Project

    Welcome! Start by creating your first project. Projects in TestRail usually represent a product or software project and serve as organizational unit for cases, results and milestones. You can create as many projects as you like.

    2/6 Add Test Cases

    Test cases represent a certain feature, behavior or functionality you wish to test. They often contain a description, a list of steps and expected results. Cases are organized in sections to make it easy to group related cases together.

    3/6 Start a Test Run

    To execute tests and enter results for your cases, you start a test run. You can have multiple test runs over time and reuse your test cases across runs. A test run has a status and you can easily follow its progress and test activity.

    4/6 Add Test Results

    Once you've added a test run, you can start testing and record results. A result has a status such as Passed or Failed and the statuses are signaled by different colors. Adding results contributes to the overall status and progress of the test run.

    5/6 Invite Team Members

    TestRail is especially useful when used with a team. Invite other team members and build your test case repository, assign test runs and record results together. Easily track the workload and progress from the Todo tab for your entire team.

    6/6 Set up Integration

    Integrate TestRail with your existing tools such as issue or bug trackers (such as Jira), requirement management tools, wiki software and many more. Collaborate with your dev team, run traceability reports and link cases & results to issues and defects.
    1. Create a project
    2. Add cases
    3. Start a run

    6. Set up integration
